sentences.text: No, the, the management of, of the-- that store-- food store--
--arranged that with the camp to change us to stronger people.
So next day, we were not going anymore out.
We were about 10, 12 days--
--a bit under two weeks.
And we were assigned another kommando.
And stronger people took over this place-- with guards, this time.
I was given another workplace where my father was with me.
And we were about 50 people in that group, taken out to the site by truck in the morning and picked up in the late afternoon.
And we had to erect three stores from scratch, from cementing the platforms, and then erect pre-fabricated walls.
And that was actually expanded later on.
We chosed tools and other equipment which they salvaged from France on their retreat.
And stuff came by trucks from the railways-- in railway carriages.
From the station, they were bringing them with trucks into that store.
We, we erected this store about-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] roughly three, four weeks it was ready, even painted green, so no, no-- it will blend in with the wood in the fields.
And when, when we started filling it, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the guards started pinching tools.
[LAUGHS] And I, I was taken with another fellow one day, pliers and, and hammers, dozens of them, into a blanket.
And the two of us took it over to a German restaurant.
And the guard took, took the money.
And the restaurateur gave us some food, some scrambled eggs.
No, the--
--the whole thing was organized, an inside job.
Only the guard, they took the money for it.
They knew about it.
They organized it.
Oh, they did it.
I only-- I did only the transportation, [LAUGHS] which was only about no no more than 200 meters from that camp, that little restaurant.
Oh, no, the guard took the money.
We-- we were happy to, to get some scrambled egg and a piece of bread.
[LAUGHS]
We can do it again next time.
[LAUGHS] Or he asks someone else to do it.
They were knocking it off.
Oh, that, that was from electric cables, quite heavy, big reels.

Some, some were reels you, you couldn't even move it-- two and a half meter in diameter, up to small tools, like hammers and, and, and pliers and saws, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nails, barbed wire-- different things, whatever--
From France.
Or whatever, whatever their post they could salvage, if they have still the time to pack them into railways and bring them into Germany.
Oh no, this was-- they, they were goods which was under German control in France.
The trucks--
--you mean?
Oh, we, we saw-- some were requisitioned French goods with German stickers on them.
And some were even cables pulled out from the ground.
They had soil on them.
It wasn't new, all of them.
We, we even had [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] insulators for carrying high-voltage transmission in in huge crates.
But we, we were there about till about September.
With me.
Now, we-- this workplace, when it was all erected and, and working, they had an office.
And they had their own guards nighttime.
Not our-- our guards was doing the pinching during the day, which was-- the, the, the, the store guard must have been looked away, or-- or disappeared during the day.
And we were taken back every night to the camp by that little truck.
They were timber-burning trucks, like saving petrol.
They had a vertical stove in the back.
And when, when they started it off, a small fan-- electric fan, from the battery-- was producing-- making the fire alive.
This thing was sealed, just a little chimney out.
And, and the wood was cut in little squares.
They had about two bags of wood on top of the truck, fill-- filling it up a few shovels of-- of that chipped wood, and close the lid, put the fan on-- electric fan.
And then it could ignite after about a minute or two.
Enough gas accumulated, wood gas.
They were quite widespread.
Even buses were running on them.
And, and you could see them.
But not so powerful, of course, as petrol.
About September, we were finished.
The camp was full of goods and erected.
They didn't needed us anymore.
And we were transferred to another camp from Rothschwaige to Allach.
We already had Dachau numbers.
I was number 71,205.
Yeah.
My father was 71206.
No.
They were given on triangle-- about five centimeters-large triangles-- one on your chest, on the left, and one on your pants, approximately near the knee, on--
--the right, outside.
Aluminum.
And they were painted red triangle, which the triangle continued partly the same width as the triangle, which carried a yellow [NON-ENGLISH], which mean Jewish.
And in that yellow thing was pressed your number--
Well, we, we had to attach it.
We had a few needles there, and that's it.
It's-- it had three little holes in the-- in the angle, which you had to--
For each angle. --put --put it under the clothes.
When I-- And this was on your striped-- on your striped--
On the striped, yeah.
And winter-- before winter, we were given some longer coats, also striped but very thin.
And the wind could blow through.
The only thing-- we got long pants.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when we came into place Allach, which was a few kilometers away, we went by trucks.
Belonged to Dachau.
Of course.
Allach was a fairly large concentration camp, only two kilometers from the main entrance of BMW.
And they were already-- the camp was divided.
We were on  the outer fringe of the camp, the furthest part from the BMW.
Between us and the German flag battery of eight cannons was only 50 meters.
And there, there were shrubs and wheat and corn between us.
When the cannons were down, you couldn't see them.
And when, when, when bombers came overhead, you could see the eighth going up in the air.
And young kids were dealing with them-- 16, 17.
And some girls were manning the projection at night.
They had, on wheels, huge projectors about a meter in diameter were catching some planes, bombers.
Well, actually, they put them so close to the-- to Allach that the camp will protect them from bombing.
But we never got bombed in the camp.
And they did.
Fighter planes did bomb them.
Some lightnings when-- gave it to them.
But that was towards the end, before the--
--end of the war.
Till about September, from, from when we arrived from Birkenau, from Dachau-- through Dachau.
I was in Allach [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about four days before liberation.
We were liberated on, on, on the transport.
I was all the time in Allach.
No.
Four days, I was liberated.
We were taken out of Allach four days before the Americans--
--took the place.
And I'll tell you after about the four days, too.
We were-- Allach was divided. 10,000 prisoners were there before us under SS guard and working in BMW, mainly.
And when we arrived on the outer camp, completely separated from the 10,000 with barbed wire, we could talk to each other through the fence.
And they were Poles, Russians, Yugoslavs, Dutch, all-- from all occupied European countries.
Not Jews.
All non-Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some Russian prisoners of war who, who had the bad luck, and same Yugoslavs, who were taken out of their military uniform and given striped thing and put in.
Because prisoners of war were, were much better handled, even the Russians.
We found out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of Allach, when, when already in the BMW, we saw Russian prisoners of war from another camp who were handling artificial fog.
Half an hour before alarms, they were given-- each had his own stations out in the bush, in the field.
And you had to open-- the fog came in 44 gallon drums with an oxy bottle next to it.
And they were buried about a half a meter in the ground.
They put a wall around them, so they won't get flooded, or whatever.
And each of these prisoners handled five or six of these.
And, and they were about 50 meters, one another.
They let the fog going before the Americans arrived with the bombers.
So BMW was completely fogged up.

If they had enough time, and they had the four-alarm-- like, for instance, when they flew over Augsburg, which was 100 kilometers, these Russian prisoners of war were given already orders, open the fog.
The fog made you a bit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coughing.
And actually, it caused more panic amongst the Germans than, than the effective-- the Americans never, never looked down on the BMW.
They were throwing the bombs in the fog.
Half of them missed.
Half of them hit.
[LAUGHS] And whatever they wanted to hit got hit.
The fog was, was very depressing for, for, for, for the people, really.
I don't know why they did it.
[LAUGHS] They-- so we, we came to that camp, and we were put into barracks with triple-- we didn't have rooms.
The whole barrack was one.
We were about maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 200 in each.
I was still with my father.
And they organized working groups the next day.
And I was in a group with my father.
We would stick together. 50 to go the other side-- through BMW, we were driven into a small camp erected for foremen, German foremen, and our-- whatever staff from BMW who were living there.
And they had-- we had to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clean potatoes for the kitchen.
And we, we built some barracks there, and even made-- we made-- before the  winter set in, we, we made walkways to each barrack made of piling, about a foot or two aboveground on little posts.
So when snow comes, you can brush the snow off between the pilings and walk dry to each barrack between the washroom and the barrack, and the dining room, and the kitchen to the barracks.
And the barracks were all spread in, in bush setting, painted dark green.
And part were occupied by night shift.
The-- those people were sleeping.
And you couldn't-- not to be disturbed.
My father had the potato shellers, 10 boys and, and him in the kitchen there-- or outside the kitchen.
And I was given with, with-- three, three were picked with two Jewish guys.
One was a German engineer from Breslau, electric engineer.
And the other was a Greek Jewish engineer from Salonika.
And the three of us, we, we were assigned in a barrack within this worker's camp which housed only electrical goods-- also belonged to the Todt Organization.
And when-- this whole setup was about a kilometer squared, maybe even less.
And our guards, which came out with us, were spread in the four corners of that square, and we were working inside.
Outside were fields, fields with potatoes, with wheat.
You could see some farmhouses about a kilometer or two away.
And that barrack which we reassigned was an empty barrack with shelves and-- to house cables and, and stuff.
And the two Germans who were in charge of that barrack only belonged to the Todt Organization, which had a different uniform from the army.
It was [INAUDIBLE] with a swastika on the red armband, and on a wide strip, written Todt Organization, which was the--
--same military organization.
T-O-D-T.
And that was-- the head of that was Speer.
You could see him two days ago on television.
It was about the Reich.
It was an evening-- fall of Berlin was, I think, Friday night or Saturday night.
But Speer was interviewed after the war.
He was the minister of, of the armament industry.
And he was in charge of the Todt Organization in, in Berlin.
But their headquarters was in Munich for, for that area.
And that-- no, as winter came and things got tougher, we were not given trucks.
After about a month, we were walking through BMW to that place, about--
It wasn't winter.
It was only getting cold, October--
--towards the end of October.
And there was no snow on the ground yet.
And we had to walk to this place and bring back the food container which was sent out to us, 50 liters for 50 people.
And when we arrived there, we dispersed, each to his own work in, in that place.
The three of us, the two engineers went in.
The two Germans were living in two rooms in that electrical goods barrack.
The, the boss, or the head of the two Germans-- one was over the other-- was a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rough man.
And, and he kept telling us to work faster.
But don't worry, because you'll never get liberated.
You'll be killed before, before anyone gets liberated.
So we knew him.
And we knew the days when he wouldn't be there.
He went to Munich.
In, in the Munich Deutsches Museum was the headquarters of, of, of this organization.
He went to get his paperwork done there in the mornings.
But normally, I had to put his bowl of water over his stove, make fire in his room.
And, and he dressed up, and washed himself, and cleared out.
And I, I find that I could bake potatoes nicely in his stove.
[LAUGHS] For his stove was going on timber or coal.
And you had the little handle which you moved, then the pot ash fall in the bottom chamber.
And I filled it with potatoes and had nice baked potatoes.
Then, of course, we found that among the electrical goods, we had this spiral resistance, which you put in a bucket, and it will boil in about 20 minutes.
It's like a kettle has inside.
Yeah, and portable, with a cable.
So we could put two in it to make it faster.
And we started doing deals with, with the other groups.
For three buckets of raw potatoes, we gave them one bucket cooked.
[LAUGHTER]
So we had the problem how to hide the potatoes.
So that was a problem.
We, we, we couldn't hide them there, really.
The other German in, in the back, his deputy, was a very leftist fellow.
He had a French girlfriend with him who came from France on the-- whenever he, he met her.
And he used to call me in and let me listen to London in the Hungarian language.
And I knew the timing, roughly.
And he let me read the paper.
He, he was a socialist, but they didn't know, maybe even a communist.
The Germans didn't know.
And he was with us there until the, the fight for Berlin started.
And then they mobilized all these Germans who were capable of still fighting, or half-invalids, and were sent off to Berlin.
Never seen him after.
No.
Can't remember his name, neither the other fellow.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the, the other-- the other fellow from the Todt, as winter came, and the-- and the bombing were closer, intensified-- Munich got bombed in 14 days and nights in-- sometimes in July.
And that's the end of, of the city.
We were taken out there on a special assignment after the third bombing.
And I was to the-- to the Hauptbahnhof given a, a bomb crater in a rainy day.
And we had the Krasna rabbi and another two with me to fill up the, the hole.
And that rabbi-- and, and, and there were guards there.
We couldn't-- we couldn't-- we were driven by Free French drivers-- so-called Free French.
They were volunteers to, to work in Germany.
And they came, and they said, no rides.
You don't go to your normal work, which at that time was still in Rothschwaige.
You go out to the central bus-- central railway station in Munich-- Hauptbahnhof-- and they will give you the instructions there.
So the roads were cut.
Fire brigades were-- pipes all over the streets.
Streets were closed.
And these French drivers, they only had the-- they knew-- only knew the main roads, which were blocked.
And each truck were open trucks.
We were about 20 on the truck with stools and had chalk numbers, from one to about 20 trucks.
So we had to remember the trucks, in case there is another alarm, so we know which truck we came to disappear from the area.
The weekend, I was already-- from 7:00-- three hours it took them to get to the Hauptbahnof.
That was only 10 kilometers away from Allach, from, from the camp.
Rothschwaige was only three kilometers from Allach, same area.
And first-- after the first night bombing, which was a British bombing, the railway was completely scattered-- trucks with axles.
The, the hood was all glass, the main hood on the-- on the station, all broken with bits of things flying.
Road-- wheels, you could see on the rooftops.
And we found, yeah, the German command in the area of the railway station, one of the foremen, or whoever it was.
He was in uniform.
He said, you can eat and drink whatever you find here.
But don't take anything with you-- same old story.
So what we found is burning local materials full of tobacco from Hungary and shoes from Romania.
But we couldn't touch the shoes.
We had wooden shoes-- wooden soles on leather shoes.
But they took us-- our shoes away.
Then we get wooden shoes.
They're very noisy when you go in the cement.
And we, we found buttermilk in 40-liter, huge stainless steel containers, opened, scattered all over the station.
But they still had-- contained about five liters because of the small neck.
Anything below the neck was about five liter in it.
People started drinking that.
Oh, they started running because you can't drink too much of that stuff.
But it was new.
Nobody got it.
No, nothing, dairy products.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] around midday, alarm came.
But there was no electricity.
So the alarm came on, on young, Hitlerjugend kids on push-bikes.
And they had the hand alarm.
One was holding the push-bike.
With the other hand, he was moving the, the alarm and going through area streets.
So the Germans got panicky.
The guards inside whistled, everybody on his own truck.
He didn't know how long that kid went around.
The bombers could be any minute here.
So we couldn't get out from the area.
And, and the bombers came.
They were maybe in groups of nine.
You could see them in the sunshine.
And one, three, five, in groups like triangles.
And three triangles, and then five, like, formed triangles, came over an area.
And the roads was full with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] women who took children in baskets and food in the front, two baskets mounted on the push-bikes.
Some had two children.
And they and other cars were blocking the road when the, the bombing started.
We just had to jump under the car and got away.
Well, next, next day, they took us in time out to a place called-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the name of the place-- outside Munich.
We had enough time to get out of there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can't remember the name now.
And we went, went until we come to the river-- Isar was the river-- on the banks of Isar.
And the German guard thought it's safe.
And when the bombers started coming towards Munich, we saw them.
But we were in a field.
And not far from us in the fields were corn fields, big flag batteries' cannons started coming up, the whole group, eight of them, and started opening fire on the things.
The guards started back on the truck.
Clear out of here because they will cop it because of their anti--
--aircraft batteries.
So we, we had about two weeks working on this Bahnhof until we got back to our normal work.
Yeah, in the railway yards, we, we had to work.
Well, mind you, first time we saw-- we, we got some biscuits and, and some lollies dropped at us from some civilian German women.
And over, over the-- they had this little walkway over the tracks.
And we were working, yeah.
But they didn't know.
They knew we were prisoners, but I don't think they knew we were Jewish.
[LAUGHS] In, in Dachau, in [NON-ENGLISH], when I was working those 10 days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German kids, boys and girls, I could see them talking.
They thought we, we were [GERMAN], criminals.
They went over to the other side of the road because we were sitting on the footpaths there having lunch, or eating our bread.
We couldn't stop them in telling them who we are, or whatever.
We just ignored them.
Yeah.
You feel that you want to survive, but you didn't care if-- practically the only time we, we had a-- we were off work in the time of the alarm.
We were just sitting.
Of course, we, we, we know this.
You know, they, they dropped silver papers.
And some-- after the raid gone, the silver papers still keep coming for 120 minutes, 130 minutes.
Those which opened up, they were flickering in the air.
But some didn't open, the bundle.
The whole bundle came down.
Some German guys took it for decorating their future Christmas tree in December,
Oh, they dropped them to, to confuse their other.
They're measuring--
--up their attitude, or whatever.
No, we, we knew roughly where, where the thing, whatever, fights on the Rhine and, and Holland border.
More or less.
Yeah.
We know were the Russians were.
I, I had these two German papers.
And one of them, the news which you read in it was two, two, three weeks old.
But you add another hundred meter for the Russians to the west, another hundred kilometers to the Americans to the east.
And more or less-- [LAUGHTER]
In Allach, that was a-- I recall that was still in the beginning.
When we arrived in Allach, were still warm days.
And did I mention this to you before?
No.
Friday night, they call it [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [GERMAN].
That's weekend.
We came home from work.
We don't go out to work.
Saturday, we work half a day.
And Saturday afternoons and Sunday, we were off.
We came in Friday afternoon.
And we had Appell.
And they-- the kapo, he was a very cruel German kapo.
His name was Knoll-- K-N-O double L, Knoll.
He, he--
His surname, yeah.
He, he had so much tattooing of chest and back.
There was not a square inch left vacant.
He was incarcerated in 1933 as a communist in Dachau when Hitler put up the first camp in Germany.
And before that, he was in Foreign Legion in, in Africa.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was the boss.
And the Lagerschreiber, which was a very nice man, he was originally from the Yugoslav foreign ministry, an, an, an officer.
He was going with the two Germans who counted us.
Yeah, he write how many people, how--
--many sick, and so on.
And he arranged that that, that hot day, we could swim.
After the Appell, they told us, you can leave your clothing on the spot where you stand.
And naked, we can walk out, march out outside the camp.
Only a hundred meters past the guards' barracks, were a big excavation of sand for a-- for a building company.
I think it was called Moll, M-O double L.
And [GERMAN] and [GERMAN], the two of them, they both were excavating sand for big construction in BMW.
And some of these were filled with water.
It was quite deep.
Some were 3, 4 meters deep, and straight deep, not gradually.
So we were allowed to swim, a whole lot of us.
Whoever was not sick, who wanted to go for a swim went for a swim.
Over about 500, 600 people went for a swim.
There was-- there was, like, five swimming pools put together, square.
And there were big cranes which took the stuff out.
When it was full of water, they dig another one next to it, send the excavation only for concreting.
When we came back-- it was splashing, so many people there.
When we came back to get the clothing, there was three people missing-- I think, two or three.
I'm not really sure.
They died.
They couldn't be accounted for.
So the Germans were annoyed that either they had to get rid of the bodies, that, or if not, they are considered escapes.
And they would be blamed.
